questionText: A 5.0 A current flows through the leads of an electrical appliance. (a) Calculate how much charge passes through the appliance in $1.0\mathrm{min}$. (b) How many electrons are in that amount of charge?
expectedAnswer: The power dissipated in the equivalent resistance is the same as the power supplied by the battery.

parts.label: a
parts.question: Calculate how much charge passes through the appliance in $1.0\mathrm{min}$.

parts.label: b
parts.question: How many electrons are in that amount of charge?

questionText: Lightning strikes. During lightning strikes from a cloud to the ground, currents as high as 25,000 A can occur and last for about $40\mu \mathrm{s}$. How much charge is transferred from the cloud to the earth during such a strike?

BIO Transmission of nerve impulses. Nerve cells transmit electrical signals through their long tubular axons. These signals propagate due to a sudden rush of $\mathrm{Na^{+}}$ ions, each with charge $+e$, into the axon. Measurements have revealed that typically about $5.6 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Na}^{+}$ ions enter each meter of the axon during a time of $10 \mathrm{~ms}$. What is the current during this inflow of charge in a meter of axon?
expectedAnswer: The resistivity ρ does not change when length or diameter are tripled.

questionText: In an ionic solution, a current consists of $\mathrm{Ca^{2+}}$ ions (of charge $+2e$) and $\mathrm{Cl^-}$ ions (of charge $-e$) traveling in opposite directions. If $5.11 \times 10^{18} \mathrm{Cl^-}$ ions go from $A$ to $B$ every $0.50 \mathrm{~min}$, while $3.24 \times 10^{18} \mathrm{Ca^{2+}}$ ions move from $B$ to $A$, what is the current (in mA) through this solution, and in which direction (from $A$ to $B$ or from $B$ to $A$) is it going?
expectedAnswer: a) False, adding resistance in parallel lowers total resistance. b) False, removing a parallel resistance increases total resistance.

questionText: A 14 gauge copper wire of diameter 1.628 mm carries a current of 12.5 mA. (a) What is the potential difference across a 2.00 m length of the wire? (b) What would the potential difference in part (a) be if the wire were silver instead of copper, but all else was the same?

parts.label: a
parts.question: What is the potential difference across a 2.00 m length of the wire?

parts.label: b
parts.question: What would the potential difference in part (a) be if the wire were silver instead of copper, but all else was the same?

questionText: You want to precut a set of 1.00 $\Omega$ strips of 14 gauge copper wire (of diameter 1.628 mm). How long should each strip be?
A wire 6.50 m long with diameter of 2.05 mm has a resistance of 0.0290 $\Omega$. What material is the wire most likely made of?
expectedAnswer: I = E/r; high-voltage batteries have larger internal resistance to prevent damage from excessive current during a short circuit.

questionText: A tightly coiled spring having 75 coils, each 3.50 cm in diameter, is made of insulated metal wire 3.25 mm in diameter. An ohmmeter connected across opposite ends of the spring reads 1.74 $\Omega$. What is the resistivity of the metal?
expectedAnswer: Energy is not all stored because some is converted to thermal energy in the resistor.

questionText: What diameter must a copper wire have if its resistance is to be the same as that of an equal length of aluminum wire with diameter 3.26 mm?
An aluminum bar 3.80 m long has a rectangular cross section 1.00 cm by 5.00 cm. (a) What is its resistance? (b) What is the length of a copper wire 1.50 mm in diameter having the same resistance?

parts.label: a
parts.question: What is its resistance?

parts.label: b
parts.question: What is the length of a copper wire 1.50 mm in diameter having the same resistance?

questionText: A steel wire of length L and radius $r_1$ has a resistance R. A second steel wire has the same length but a radius $r_2$ and a resistance of 3R. Find the ratio $r_1 / r_2$.

A ductile metal wire has resistance R. What will be the resistance of this wire in terms of R if it is stretched to three times its original length, assuming that the density and resistivity of the material do not change when the wire is stretched? (Hint: The amount of metal does not change, so stretching out the wire will affect its cross-sectional area.)

What is the resistance of a Nichrome wire at $0.0^{\circ}C$ if its resistance is 100.00 $\Omega$ at $11.5^{\circ}C$? The temperature coefficient of resistivity for Nichrome is 0.00040 $(C^{\circ})^{-1}$.

A 1.50 m cylindrical rod of diameter 0.500 cm is connected to a power supply that maintains a constant potential difference of 15.0 V across its ends, while an ammeter measures the current through it. You observe that at room temperature ($20.0^{\circ}C$) the ammeter reads 18.5 A, while at $92.0^{\circ}C$ it reads 17.2 A. You can ignore any thermal expansion of the rod. Find (a) the resistivity and (b) the temperature coefficient of resistivity at $20^{\circ}C$ for the material of the rod.

parts.label: a
parts.question: the resistivity

parts.label: b
parts.question: the temperature coefficient of resistivity at $20^{\circ}C$ for the material of the rod.

questionText: An iron wire has a temperature coefficient of resistivity of +0.0050 $(C^{\circ})^{-1}$. When it is placed into boiling water, it has a resistance of 1000 $\Omega$. What is its resistance when it is placed into an ice-water bath?

In a laboratory experiment, you vary the current through an object and measure the resulting potential difference across it in each case. Figure 19.52 shows a graph of this potential V as a function of the current I. (a) Does Ohm's law apply to this object? Why do you say so? (b) How is the resistance of the object related to the slope of the graph? Show why. (c) Use the slope of the graph to find the resistance of the object.
expectedAnswer: a) Yes, it obeys Ohm's law  b) Slope of V vs. I graph  c) 150 Ω

parts.label: a
parts.question: Does Ohm's law apply to this object? Why do you say so?

parts.label: b
parts.question: How is the resistance of the object related to the slope of the graph? Show why.

parts.label: c
parts.question: Use the slope of the graph to find the resistance of the object.

questionText:
DATA The following measurements of current and potential difference were made on a resistor constructed of Nichrome wire, where $V_{ab}$ is the potential difference across the wire and I is the current through it:

| I(A) | $V_{ab}$(V) |
| --- | --- |
| 0.50 | 1.94 |
| 1.00 | 3.88 |
| 2.00 | 7.76 |
| 4.00 | 15.52 |

(a) Graph $V_{ab}$ as a function of I. (b) Does Ohm's law apply to Nichrome? How can you tell? (c) What is the resistance of the resistor in ohms?
expectedAnswer: b) Yes, it obeys Ohm's law  c) 3.88 Ω

parts.label: a
parts.question: Graph $V_{ab}$ as a function of I.

parts.label: b
parts.question: Does Ohm's law apply to Nichrome? How can you tell?

parts.label: c
parts.question: What is the resistance of the resistor in ohms?

questionText: When you connect an unknown resistor across the terminals of a 1.50 V AAA battery having negligible internal resistance, you measure a current of 18.0 mA flowing through it. (a) What is the resistance of this resistor? (b) If you now place the resistor across the terminals of a 12.6 V car battery having no internal resistance, how much current will flow? (c) You now put the resistor across the terminals of an unknown battery of negligible internal resistance and measure a current of 0.453 A flowing through it. What is the potential difference across the terminals of the battery?

parts.label: a
parts.question: What is the resistance of this resistor?

parts.label: b
parts.question: If you now place the resistor across the terminals of a 12.6 V car battery having no internal resistance, how much current will flow?

parts.label: c
parts.question: You now put the resistor across the terminals of an unknown battery of negligible internal resistance and measure a current of 0.453 A flowing through it. What is the potential difference across the terminals of the battery?

questionText: BIO Current in the body. The resistance of the body varies from approximately 500 k$\Omega$ (when it is very dry) to about 1 k$\Omega$ (when it is wet). The maximum safe current is about 5.0 mA. At 10 mA or above, muscle contractions can occur that may be fatal. What is the largest potential difference that a person can safely touch if his body is wet? Is this result within the range of common household voltages?
A copper transmission cable 100 km long and 10.0 cm in diameter carries a current of 125 A. What is the potential drop across the cable?
A gold wire 6.40 m long and of diameter 0.840 mm carries a current of 1.15 A. Find (a) the resistance of this wire and (b) the potential difference between its ends.

parts.label: a
parts.question: the resistance of this wire

parts.label: b
parts.question: the potential difference between its ends.

questionText: A steel wire, of length L and diameter D, has a resistance of 100 $\Omega$. What current would flow through a silver wire of the same diameter and the same length if a 10.0 V potential difference were applied across its length?
A 6.00 V lantern battery is connected to a 10.5 $\Omega$ lightbulb, and the resulting current in the circuit is 0.350 A. What is the internal resistance of the battery?

When the switch in Figure 19.53 is open, the voltmeter V across the battery reads 3.08 V. When the switch is closed, the voltmeter reading drops to 2.97 V and the ammeter A reads 1.65 A. Find the emf, the internal resistance of the battery, and the circuit resistance R. Assume that the two meters are ideal, so that they don't affect the circuit.

A nonideal 10.0 V battery is connected across a resistor R. The internal resistance of the battery is 2.0 $\Omega$. Calculate the potential difference across the resistor for the following values: (a) R = 100 $\Omega$, (b) $R = 10\Omega$ (c) $R = 2\Omega$ (d) Find the current through the battery for all three cases.

parts.label: a
parts.question: R = 100 $\Omega$

parts.label: b
parts.question: $R = 10\Omega$

parts.label: c
parts.question: $R = 2\Omega$

parts.label: d
parts.question: Find the current through the battery for all three cases.

questionText: With a $1500\mathrm{M}\Omega$ resistor across its terminals, the terminal voltage of a certain battery is $2.50\mathrm{V}$. With only a $5.00\Omega$ resistor across its terminals, the terminal voltage is $1.75\mathrm{V}$ (a) Find the internal emf and the internal resistance of this battery. (b) What would be the terminal voltage if the $5.00\Omega$ resistor were replaced by a $7.00\Omega$ resistor?

parts.label: a
parts.question: Find the internal emf and the internal resistance of this battery.

parts.label: b
parts.question: What would be the terminal voltage if the $5.00\Omega$ resistor were replaced by a $7.00\Omega$ resistor?

questionText: An automobile starter motor is connected to a $12.0\mathrm{V}$ battery. When the starter is activated it draws $150\mathrm{A}$ of current, and the battery voltage drops to $7.0\mathrm{V}$. What is the battery's internal resistance?

Consider the circuit shown in Figure 19.54. The terminal voltage of the $24.0\mathrm{V}$ battery is $21.2\mathrm{V}$. What is (a) the internal resistance $r$ of the battery; (b) the resistance $R$ of the circuit resistor?

questionText: Power rating of a resistor. The power rating of a resistor is the maximum power it can safely dissipate without being damaged by overheating. (a) If the power rating of a certain $15\mathrm{k}\Omega$ resistor is $5.0\mathrm{W}$, what is the maximum current it can carry without damage? What is the greatest allowable potential difference across the terminals of this resistor? (b) If a $9.0\mathrm{k}\Omega$ resistor is to be connected across a $120\mathrm{V}$ potential difference, what power rating is required for that resistor?

If the voltmeter reads $11.3\mathrm{V}$: (a) how much power is being dissipated by the appliance, and (b) what is the internal resistance of the battery?

questionText: BIO Treatment of heart failure. A heart defibrillator is used to enable the heart to start beating if it has stopped. This is done by passing a large current of 12 A through the body at $25\mathrm{V}$ for a very short time, usually about $3.0~\mathrm{ms}$. (a) What power does the defibrillator deliver to the body, and (b) how much energy is transferred?

parts.label: a
parts.question: What power does the defibrillator deliver to the body

parts.question: how much energy is transferred?

questionText: Lightbulb. The wattage rating of a lightbulb is the power it consumes when it is connected across a $120\mathrm{V}$ potential difference. For example, a $60\mathrm{W}$ lightbulb consumes $60.0\mathrm{W}$ of electric power only when it is connected across a $120\mathrm{V}$ potential difference. (a) What is the resistance of a $60\mathrm{W}$ lightbulb? (b) Without doing any calculations, would you expect a $100\mathrm{W}$ bulb to have more or less resistance than a $60\mathrm{W}$ bulb? Calculate and find out.

questionText: BIO Electrical safety. This procedure is not recommended! You'll see why after you work the problem. You are on an aluminum ladder that is standing on the ground, trying to fix an electrical connection with a metal screwdriver that has a metal handle. Your body is wet because you are sweating from the exertion; therefore, it has a resistance of $1.0\mathrm{k}\Omega$. (a) If you accidentally touch the "hot" wire connected to the $120\mathrm{V}$ line, how much current will pass through your body? Is this amount enough to be dangerous? (The maximum safe current is about $5\mathrm{mA}$.) (b) How much electric power is delivered to your body?

parts.label: a

parts.question: How much electric power is delivered to your body?

questionText: BIO Electric eels. Electric eels generate electric pulses along their skin that can be used to stun an enemy when they come into contact with it. Tests have shown that these pulses can be up to $500\mathrm{V}$ and produce currents of $80\mathrm{mA}$ (or even larger). A typical pulse lasts for $10\mathrm{ms}$. What power and how much energy are delivered to the unfortunate enemy with a single pulse, assuming a steady current?

A $500\Omega$ and a $200\Omega$ resistor are connected in series with an ideal battery that has an emf of $20\mathrm{V}$. (a) What current flows through each resistor? (b) What power is delivered to each resistor? (c) What power is supplied by the battery?

parts.question: What power is delivered to each resistor?

parts.question: What power is supplied by the battery?

Find the average current that this cell phone draws when turned on.

parts.label: b
parts.question: the terminal voltage $V_{ab}$ of the $16.0\mathrm{V}$ battery.

questionText: If an ohmmeter is connected between points $a$ and $b$ in each of the circuits shown in Figure 19.68, what will it read?

A refrigerator draws 3.5 A of current while operating on a 120 V power line. If the refrigerator runs 50% of the time and electric power costs $0.12 per kWh, how much does it cost to run this refrigerator for a 30-day month?

A toaster using a Nichrome heating element operates on 120 V. When it is switched on at 20°C, the heating element carries an initial current of 1.35 A. A few seconds later, the current reaches the steady value of 1.23 A. (a) What is the final temperature of the element? The average value of the temperature coefficient of resistivity for Nichrome over the temperature range from 20°C to the final temperature of the element is $4.5 \times 10^{-4} (\mathrm{C}^{\circ})^{-1}$. (b) What is the power dissipated in the heating element (i) initially; (ii) when the current reaches a steady value?

parts.label: a
parts.question: What is the final temperature of the element? The average value of the temperature coefficient of resistivity for Nichrome over the temperature range from 20°C to the final temperature of the element is $4.5 \times 10^{-4} (\mathrm{C}^{\circ})^{-1}$.

parts.label: b
parts.question: What is the power dissipated in the heating element (i) initially; (ii) when the current reaches a steady value?

questionText: A piece of wire has a resistance $R$. It is cut into three pieces of equal length, and the pieces are twisted together parallel to each other. What is the resistance of the resulting wire in terms of $R$?

Flashlight batteries. A typical small flashlight contains two batteries, each having an emf of 1.5 V, connected in series with a bulb having resistance 17 Ω. (a) If the internal resistance of the batteries is negligible, what power is delivered to the bulb? (b) If the batteries last for 5.0 h, what is the total energy delivered to the bulb? (c) The resistance of real batteries increases as they run down. If the initial internal resistance is negligible, what is the combined internal resistance of both batteries when the power to the bulb has decreased to half its initial value? (Assume that the resistance of the bulb is constant. Actually, it will change somewhat when the current through the filament changes because this changes the temperature of the filament and hence the resistivity of the filament wire.)

parts.label: a
parts.question: If the internal resistance of the batteries is negligible, what power is delivered to the bulb?

parts.label: b
parts.question: If the batteries last for 5.0 h, what is the total energy delivered to the bulb?

parts.label: c
parts.question: The resistance of real batteries increases as they run down. If the initial internal resistance is negligible, what is the combined internal resistance of both batteries when the power to the bulb has decreased to half its initial value?

questionText: In the circuit of Figure 19.69, find (a) the current through the $8.0\,\Omega$ resistor, (b) the total rate of dissipation of electrical energy in the $8.0\,\Omega$ resistor and in the internal resistance of the batteries, and (c) the total power being delivered by the two batteries.

parts.label: a
parts.question: the current through the $8.0\,\Omega$ resistor

parts.label: b
parts.question: the total rate of dissipation of electrical energy in the $8.0\,\Omega$ resistor and in the internal resistance of the batteries

parts.label: c
parts.question: the total power being delivered by the two batteries

questionText: BIO Struck by lightning. Lightning strikes can involve currents as high as 25,000 A that last for about $40\,\mu\mathrm{s}$. If a person is struck by a bolt of lightning with these properties, the current will pass through his body. We shall assume that his mass is $75\,\mathrm{kg}$, that he is wet (after all, he is in a rainstorm) and therefore has a resistance of $1.0\,\mathrm{k}\Omega$, and that his body is all water (which is reasonable for a rough, but plausible, approximation). (a) By how many degrees Celsius would this lightning bolt increase the temperature of 75 kg of water? (b) Given that the internal body temperature is about $37^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, would the person's temperature actually increase that much? Why or why not? What would happen first?
expectedAnswer: a) 80 C°  b) Part of the person's body will be vaporized.

parts.label: a
parts.question: By how many degrees Celsius would this lightning bolt increase the temperature of 75 kg of water?

parts.label: b
parts.question: Given that the internal body temperature is about $37^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, would the person's temperature actually increase that much? Why or why not? What would happen first?

questionText: Each of the three resistors in Figure 19.70 has a resistance of $2.00\,\Omega$ and can dissipate a maximum of $32.0\,\mathrm{W}$ without becoming excessively heated. What is the maximum power the circuit can dissipate?

Energy use of home appliances. An 1800 W toaster, a 1400 W electric frying pan, and a 75 W lamp are plugged into the same electrical outlet in a 20 A, 120 V circuit. (Note: When plugged into the same outlet, the three devices are in parallel with each other across the 120 V outlet.) (a) What current is drawn by each device? (b) Will this combination blow the circuit breaker?
expectedAnswer: a) Toaster: 15.0 A, Frypan: 11.7 A, Lamp: 0.625 A  b) 27.3 A, circuit breaker will blow

parts.label: a
parts.question: What current is drawn by each device?

parts.label: b
parts.question: Will this combination blow the circuit breaker?

questionText: Two identical 1.00 Ω wires are laid side by side and soldered together so that they touch each other for half of their lengths. (See Figure 19.71.) What is the equivalent resistance of this combination?

Three identical resistors are connected in series. When a certain potential difference is applied across the combination, the total power dissipated is 27 W. What power would be dissipated if the three resistors were connected in parallel across the same potential difference?

(a) Calculate the equivalent resistance of the circuit of Figure 19.72 between $x$ and $y$. (b) If a voltmeter is connected between points $a$ and $x$ when the current in the $8.0\,\Omega$ resistor is 2.4 A in the direction from left to right in the figure, what will it read?

parts.label: a
parts.question: Calculate the equivalent resistance of the circuit of Figure 19.72 between $x$ and $y$.

parts.label: b
parts.question: If a voltmeter is connected between points $a$ and $x$ when the current in the $8.0\,\Omega$ resistor is 2.4 A in the direction from left to right in the figure, what will it read?

questionText: A power plant transmits $150\,\mathrm{kW}$ of power to a nearby town through wires that have a total resistance of 0.25 Ω. What percentage of the power is dissipated as heat in the wire if the power is transmitted at (a) 220 V and (b) 22 kV?

parts.label: b
parts.question: 22 kV

questionText: What must the emf $\mathcal{E}$ in Figure 19.73 be in order for the current through the $7.00\,\Omega$ resistor to be 1.80 A? Each emf source has negligible internal resistance.
expectedAnswer: a) 9.98 × 10^3 J  b) 5.54 × 10^3 J  c) Isobaric  d) Isobaric (Q = 2.49 × 10^4 J vs Q = 0)

questionText: For the circuit shown in Figure 19.74, if a voltmeter is connected across points $a$ and $b$, (a) what will it read, and (b) which point is at a higher potential, $a$ or $b$?

parts.label: a
parts.question: what will it read

parts.label: b
parts.question: which point is at a higher potential, $a$ or $b$?

questionText: A 4600 Ω resistor is connected across a charged 0.800 nF capacitor. The initial current through the resistor, just after the connection is made, is measured to be 0.250 A. (a) What magnitude of charge was initially on each plate of this capacitor? (b) How long after the connection is made will it take before the charge is reduced to $1 / e$ of its maximum value?

parts.label: a
parts.question: What magnitude of charge was initially on each plate of this capacitor?

parts.label: b
parts.question: How long after the connection is made will it take before the charge is reduced to $1 / e$ of its maximum value?

questionText: A capacitor that is initially uncharged is connected in series with a resistor and a $400.0\mathrm{V}$ emf source with negligible internal resistance. Just after the circuit is completed, the current through the resistor is $0.800\mathrm{mA}$ and the time constant for the circuit is $6.00\mathrm{s}$. What are (a) the resistance of the resistor and (b) the capacitance of the capacitor?

parts.label: a
parts.question: the resistance of the resistor

parts.label: b
parts.question: the capacitance of the capacitor

questionText: In the circuit shown in Figure 19.75, $R$ is a variable resistor whose value can range from 0 to $\infty$, and $a$ and $b$ are the terminals of a battery having an emf $\mathcal{E} = 15.0\mathrm{V}$ and an internal resistance of $4.00\Omega$. The ammeter and voltmeter are both idealized meters. As $R$ varies over its full range of values, what will be the largest and smallest readings of (a) the voltmeter and (b) the ammeter? (c) Sketch qualitative graphs of the readings of both meters as functions of $R$, as $R$ ranges from 0 to $\infty$.

parts.label: a
parts.question: the largest and smallest readings of the voltmeter

parts.label: b
parts.question: the largest and smallest readings of the ammeter

parts.label: c
parts.question: Sketch qualitative graphs of the readings of both meters as functions of $R$, as $R$ ranges from 0 to $\infty$.

questionText: What is the best explanation for the behavior exhibited in the data?
choices: Longer threads can carry more current than shorter threads and so make better electrical conductors.
The thread stops being a conductor when it is stretched to 13 mm due to breaks that occur in the thin coating.
As the thread is stretched, the coating thins and its resistance increases; as the thread is relaxed, the coating returns nearly to its original state.
The resistance of the thread increases with distance from the end of the thread.

questionText: If the conductivity of the thread results from the aqueous coating only, how does the cross-sectional area $A$ of the coating compare when the thread is $13\mathrm{mm}$ long versus the starting length of $5\mathrm{mm}$? Assume that the resistivity of the coating remains constant and the coating is uniform along the thread. $A_{13\mathrm{mm}}$ is about
What is the maximum current that flows in the thread during this experiment if the voltage source is a $9\mathrm{V}$ battery?

In another experiment, a piece of the web is suspended so that it can move freely. When either a positively charged object or a negatively charged object is brought near the web, the thread is observed to move toward the charged object. What is the best interpretation of this observation? The web is
choices: either a positively or negatively charged conductor.

questionText: Assume that a typical open ion channel spanning an axon's membrane has a resistance of $1 \times 10^{11} \Omega$. We can model this ion channel, with its pore, as a $12 \mathrm{~nm}$-long cylinder of radius $0.3 \mathrm{~nm}$. What is the resistivity of the fluid in the pore?

In a simple model of an axon conducting a nerve signal, ions move across the cell membrane through open ion channels, which act as purely resistive elements. If a typical current density (current per unit cross-sectional area) in the cell membrane is $5\mathrm{mA} / \mathrm{cm}^2$ when the voltage across the membrane (the action potential) is $50\mathrm{mV}$, what is the number density of open ion channels in the membrane?

Cell membranes across a wide variety of organisms have a capacitance per unit area of $1\mu \mathrm{F} / \mathrm{cm}^2$. For the electrical signal in a nerve to propagate down the axon, the charge on the membrane "capacitor" must change. What time constant is required when the ion channels are open?
